questions.question: Fine-tuning process involves freezing rows just like Feature Extraction does, but it only freezes the last (newest) few layers.
questions.explanation: Fine-tuning freezes the EARLY layers (not the last few). It keeps the learned features from the pre-trained model's early layers and allows later layers to adapt to the new task.

questions.question: Although fine-tuning uses a pre-trained model for the first multiple layers of the model, it also needs a different/new...
options: set of kernels for the first few layers (that detects simple shapes) in the Feature Extraction step
dataset
architecture for the final dense layers
optimizer and loss function
questions.explanation: Fine-tuning requires a new dataset for the specific task you're adapting the model to, while reusing the learned features from the pre-trained model.

questions.question: Why don't we fine-tune the earlier layers that perform Feature Extraction?
options: Because it is mathematically impossible to fine-tune the earlier layers
Fine-tuning is a process that cannot be mixed with Feature Extraction
Because simple shapes can be learned generally from any dataset AND to prevent overparameterization and overfitting
To ensure that we don't end up with too many layers in the model
questions.explanation: Early layers learn general features (edges, shapes) applicable across datasets. Fine-tuning them risks overfitting and overparameterization since they already capture useful general patterns.

questions.question: Visualizing intermediate convnet intermediate activations is synonymous to...
options: Visualizing the kernels themselves, overlain on top of the original data
Visualizing the heat map
Visualizing the Feature Map
Visualizing the filters themselves, overlain on top of the original data
questions.explanation: Visualizing intermediate activations shows the Feature Maps - the output of convolutional layers showing which patterns/features were detected at each stage.

questions.question: What technique of visualization is especially helpful when trying to fix a model's incorrect classification of images?
options: Filters
Feature Maps
Heatmaps
Plot of x-y comparing 2 features
questions.explanation: Heatmaps show which parts of an image the model focused on when making predictions, helping identify why it misclassified (e.g., focusing on background instead of the object).

questions.question: What aspect of the convnet is multiplied with the data of the previous layer to emphasize certain features in the image?
options: Conv base
Activation function
Feature Map
Kernel (or a set of kernels - a filter)
questions.explanation: Kernels (or filters composed of multiple kernels) are convolved with the previous layer's output to detect and emphasize specific features like edges or patterns.

questions.question: It is hard to understand the significance/meaning of a heat map that is not overlaid on the original image.
questions.explanation: Heat maps show activation intensity but need the original image context to understand WHAT the model was focusing on (object, background, specific features).

questions.question: Data augmentation is a technique used to generate new training data by applying random transformations to existing samples.
questions.explanation: Data augmentation creates variations of training images through transformations (rotation, flipping, cropping) to increase dataset diversity and reduce overfitting.

questions.question: When using a pretrained convolutional neural network, you should always use all layers of the original model, including the final classification layer.
questions.explanation: The final classification layer is specific to the original task and should be replaced. You typically keep the convolutional base and add new dense layers for your specific task.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of data augmentation?
options: To increase computational speed
To reduce model complexity
To create entirely new images
To prevent overfitting and improve generalization
questions.explanation: Data augmentation artificially expands the training set with transformed versions of existing images, helping the model generalize better and reducing overfitting.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a common data augmentation technique?
options: Rotation
Deleting pixels
Flipping
Color jittering
questions.explanation: Deleting pixels is not a standard augmentation technique. Common methods include rotation, flipping, cropping, scaling, and color jittering.

questions.question: When using feature extraction with a pretrained model, what part of the original model is typically used?
options: Only the densely connected layers
The convolutional base (early layers)
Random layers from the middle of the network
Only the final classification layer
questions.explanation: Feature extraction uses the convolutional base (early/middle layers) which learned general features, and replaces the final dense layers with new ones for your specific task.

questions.question: When deciding how many layers to use from a pretrained model, what should you consider?
options: Only use layers that have been fine-tuned previously
Never use any layers from the original model
Use only early layers if the new dataset is very different from the original
Always use all layers regardless of dataset similarity
questions.explanation: If your dataset differs significantly from the pre-trained model's original dataset, use fewer layers (only early ones) since later layers learn task-specific features.

questions.question: In convolutional neural networks, the depth (number of channels/filters) typically decreases as you progress through deeper layers.
questions.explanation: Depth (number of filters) typically INCREASES in deeper layers as the network learns increasingly complex features, while spatial dimensions decrease through pooling.

questions.question: Convolutional layers are translation-invariant, meaning they can recognize a pattern regardless of where it appears in an image.
questions.explanation: CNNs learn patterns that can be detected anywhere in the image due to weight sharing and sliding convolution operations, making them translation-invariant.

questions.question: What is the main difference between densely connected layers and convolutional layers?
options: Dense layers are faster to compute than convolutional layers
Dense layers learn local patterns while convolutional layers learn global
Dense layers produce feature maps while convolutional layers produce weight matrices
Dense layers learn global patterns while convolutional layers learn local patterns
questions.explanation: Convolutional layers learn local spatial patterns through small receptive fields, while dense layers connect to all inputs learning global patterns across the entire feature space.

questions.question: In a CNN architecture, what is the purpose of max pooling?
options: To introduce more trainable parameters
To downsample feature maps and reduce their size
To increase the size of feature maps
To apply a learned linear transformation to the input
questions.explanation: Max pooling downsamples feature maps by taking the maximum value in each pooling window, reducing spatial dimensions while retaining important features.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes a 'feature map' in the context of CNNs?
options: The combined output of all layers in a CNN
The input tensor before applying convolution
A 2D spatial map showing how strongly a filter detected patterns across the input
The set of weights in a convolutional kernel
questions.explanation: A feature map is the output of applying a filter to an input, showing the spatial locations where specific patterns were detected with varying intensities.

questions.question: Why are CNNs considered data-efficient for perceptual problems like image recognition?
options: They don't need to be trained on large datasets
They always require data augmentation
They learn local, translation-invariant features
They require more parameters than other neural networks
questions.explanation: CNNs learn local patterns that can be reused across the entire image (translation-invariance) and across different images, requiring fewer parameters and less data than fully connected networks.

questions.question: Reinforcement learning...
options: Uses labeled examples to adjust model parameters
Trains on self-generated labels to learn representations
Finds structure in data without explicit labels
Learns by trial and error through environment interaction
questions.explanation: Reinforcement learning learns through trial and error, receiving rewards or penalties for actions in an environment to develop optimal strategies.

questions.question: You have a client with a closet full of tools but no dataset. Which model should you use to save time?
options: Reinforcement Learning
Unsupervised Learning
Supervised Learning
Self-Supervised Learning
questions.explanation: Unsupervised Learning finds patterns in unlabeled data, making it ideal when you have data (tools) but no labels, saving time on data labeling.

questions.question: You need AI software for a drone to navigate downtown Toronto. What type of model should you use?
options: Supervised Learning
Self-Supervised Learning
Unsupervised Learning
Reinforcement Learning
questions.explanation: Reinforcement Learning is ideal for navigation tasks where the agent (drone) learns optimal actions through interaction with the environment, receiving rewards for good navigation.

questions.question: You need software to analyze a customer database and predict their next purchase. Which type of model should you use?
options: Reinforcement Learning
Self-Supervised Learning
Unsupervised Learning
Supervised Learning
questions.explanation: Supervised Learning is best for prediction tasks when you have labeled historical data (past purchases) to train the model to predict future outcomes.

questions.question: What is regularization?
options: The weights are subtracted from one another and then the average used
The weights are multiplied by a constant and/or squared, reducing fluctuations in the loss function
It increases fluctuations in the loss function graph
It ensures the weights are between 0-1
questions.explanation: Regularization adds a penalty term (like L1 or L2) based on weight magnitudes to the loss function, discouraging large weights and reducing overfitting by smoothing the loss function.

questions.question: If the loss function graph immediately starts to overfit, what will you see?
options: A quick increase in loss of the train set
A sharp increase in loss in the test set AND a quick decrease in loss of the train set
A sharp decrease in loss in the test set
under-parameterization
questions.explanation: Overfitting shows as diverging train/test loss: training loss decreases (model memorizing) while test loss increases (poor generalization), often with over-parameterization.

questions.question: You cannot use Cross validation and Regularization at the same time.
questions.explanation: Cross-validation and regularization are complementary techniques. Cross-validation evaluates performance, while regularization prevents overfitting. They're often used together.

questions.question: You cannot use both L1 and L2 regularization on the same model.
questions.explanation: You can use both L1 and L2 regularization together (called Elastic Net regularization), combining their benefits: L1 for feature selection and L2 for weight shrinkage.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of transfer learning?
options: It eliminates the need for any training data
It always produces more accurate models than training from scratch
It leverages pre-learned features, requiring less data and training time
It only works for image classification tasks
questions.explanation: Transfer learning reuses knowledge from pre-trained models, significantly reducing the amount of data and computation needed to achieve good performance on new tasks.

questions.question: What is the difference between feature extraction and fine-tuning in transfer learning?
options: They are the same technique with different names
Fine-tuning freezes all layers while feature extraction trains all layers
Feature extraction freezes the convolutional base, fine-tuning allows some layers to train
Feature extraction is only for CNNs, fine-tuning is for all models
questions.explanation: Feature extraction freezes the pre-trained layers and only trains new layers. Fine-tuning unfreezes some later pre-trained layers to adapt them to the new task.

questions.question: Why is a smaller learning rate typically used when fine-tuning?
options: To speed up training
To increase model accuracy immediately
To avoid drastically changing the pre-trained weights
To reduce memory usage
questions.explanation: A smaller learning rate prevents large updates that would destroy the useful features already learned by the pre-trained model, making gradual, careful adjustments.

questions.question: What does L1 regularization (Lasso) tend to do to model weights?
options: Increases all weights equally
Makes weights very large
Drives some weights exactly to zero (feature selection)
Only affects the learning rate
questions.explanation: L1 regularization adds the absolute value of weights to the loss, tending to drive some weights exactly to zero, effectively performing feature selection.

questions.question: What does L2 regularization (Ridge) tend to do to model weights?
options: Drives weights to zero completely
Shrinks weights toward zero but rarely exactly to zero
Only affects bias terms
Increases weight variance
questions.explanation: L2 regularization adds the squared value of weights to the loss, shrinking all weights toward (but rarely to) zero, preventing any single weight from becoming too large.

questions.question: What is dropout in the context of neural networks?
options: Removing entire layers from the network
Stopping training when validation loss increases
Randomly setting a fraction of neurons to zero during training
Deleting training examples with errors
questions.explanation: Dropout randomly deactivates a percentage of neurons during each training iteration, preventing co-adaptation and reducing overfitting.

questions.question: When should you consider using data augmentation?
options: Only when you have too much data
When you have limited training data and want to prevent overfitting
Only for text data, not images
When your model is already performing perfectly
questions.explanation: Data augmentation is most beneficial when training data is limited, as it artificially increases dataset diversity and helps the model generalize better.

questions.question: What is early stopping?
options: Training for a fixed small number of epochs
Stopping training when validation performance stops improving
Ending training when the model reaches 100% training accuracy
Randomly stopping training at any point
questions.explanation: Early stopping monitors validation performance and halts training when it stops improving, preventing overfitting before it occurs.

questions.question: What is batch normalization?
options: Normalizing only the input layer
Normalizing the outputs of layers to stabilize and speed up training
Adjusting batch sizes during training
Normalizing only the final layer's output
questions.explanation: Batch normalization normalizes layer inputs across the mini-batch, reducing internal covariate shift and allowing higher learning rates and faster training.

questions.question: What is the vanishing gradient problem?
options: Gradients becoming too large and causing instability
Gradients becoming extremely small in early layers, slowing learning
Gradients disappearing completely after one epoch
Gradients only affecting certain layers
questions.explanation: In deep networks, gradients can become exponentially smaller as they backpropagate through layers, making early layers learn very slowly or not at all.

questions.question: What is the purpose of using validation data?
options: To increase the training dataset size
To tune hyperparameters and detect overfitting during training
To replace the test dataset
To train the final model
questions.explanation: Validation data helps tune hyperparameters, select the best model, and monitor for overfitting during training without touching the test set.

questions.question: Why do we typically normalize input images before feeding them to a CNN?
options: To make all images the same size
To ensure pixel values are in a consistent range (e.g., 0-1), helping training converge faster
To reduce file size
To convert images to grayscale
questions.explanation: Normalizing inputs (scaling pixel values to 0-1 or standardizing) helps gradients flow better and allows the model to converge faster and more stably.

questions.question: What is the receptive field of a neuron in a CNN?
options: The number of parameters in the layer
The region of the input image that affects that neuron's activation
The size of the kernel
The number of filters in the layer
questions.explanation: The receptive field is the area of the original input that influences a particular neuron's output, growing larger in deeper layers through successive convolutions.

questions.question: What is the purpose of using padding in convolutional layers?
options: To increase the number of channels
To preserve spatial dimensions and prevent information loss at borders
To reduce computational cost
To apply regularization
questions.explanation: Padding adds zeros around the input border, allowing the output to maintain the same spatial dimensions as the input and preventing loss of edge information.

questions.question: What is stride in a convolutional layer?
options: The size of the kernel
The number of steps the kernel moves across the input
The depth of the output
The learning rate
questions.explanation: Stride determines how many pixels the convolutional kernel shifts at each step. Larger strides reduce output dimensions more aggressively than smaller strides.

questions.question: What is the difference between same padding and valid padding?
options: Same padding reduces size, valid padding maintains size
They are the same thing
Same padding maintains input size, valid padding uses no padding (size reduces)
Same padding only works with odd kernel sizes
questions.explanation: 'Same' padding adds enough zeros to keep output size equal to input size. 'Valid' padding uses no padding, resulting in smaller output dimensions.

questions.question: What is global average pooling?
options: Averaging all values in the entire feature map to produce one value per channel
Taking the average of only the maximum values
Pooling only specific regions of the feature map
The same as max pooling
questions.explanation: Global average pooling averages all spatial positions in each feature map, reducing each channel to a single value, often used to replace dense layers at the end of CNNs.

questions.question: What is the purpose of using multiple filters in a convolutional layer?
options: To reduce overfitting
To detect different types of features (edges, textures, patterns) simultaneously
To increase image resolution
To reduce computational cost
questions.explanation: Each filter learns to detect different features. Using multiple filters allows the layer to detect various patterns simultaneously, creating multiple feature maps.
